summary: Julia Evans shares her strategies for getting useful feedback from internet comments on her blog posts, rather than simply avoiding comments altogether. She advocates writing about facts and personal stories rather than opinions, which naturally steers discussion toward constructive territory. She describes tactics like asking technical questions she doesn't know the answer to, preempting common suggestions, and avoiding flamebait topics. She also discusses setting boundaries on social media, analyzing negative comments for useful insights, and refusing to engage in arguments.
key_points: Writing about facts and personal experiences generates more constructive comments than writing about opinions
Asking technical questions you genuinely don't know the answer to creates a win-win dynamic where commenters get to share knowledge and the author learns
Preemptively addressing common suggestions and listing things you chose not to do prevents repetitive advice and helps readers learn about alternatives
Avoiding known flamebait topics and boring recurring debates helps maintain comment quality, though sometimes important topics are worth the noise
Negative comments can be analyzed for useful signal — criticism that seems unfair may reveal genuine gaps in your explanation
money_quotes: I've learned a huge amount from reading internet comments on my posts from strangers over the years, even if sometimes people are jerks.

This is fun because it feels like a guaranteed way to get value out of people's comments – people LOVE answering questions, and so they get to look smart, and I get the answer to a question I have! Everyone wins!
I've found that asking people to share their experiences instead of their opinions leads to a lot of useful information and discussion.
I have a weird catalog in my head of things not to mention if I don't want to start the same discussion about that thing for the 50th time.
I think it's very important on social media to occasionally set some rules about what kind of behaviour I won't tolerate.
I dislike arguing on the internet and I'm extremely bad at it, so it's not a good use of my time.
I realized that maybe the commenters didn't think it was easy or obvious, and wanted to know how to do it. Which makes sense! It's not obvious at all if you haven't done it before!
I promise this is not at all the case and I have 100000 feelings about everything that happens on the internet and get upset all the time.
key_insight: You can shape internet discussion toward useful, constructive feedback by writing about facts and experiences rather than opinions, asking genuine questions, and strategically avoiding flamebait topics.
